topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the waiting room was thick, pressing down on Elias’s chest until it felt like a physical weight. He traced the pattern on the worn fabric of his jeans, his gaze fixed on a scuff mark on the linoleum floor. Every time a door clicked, his shoulders tensed, a familiar knot tightening beneath his collar. He hadn't expected to see him. Not here. Dr. Silas Vance, the architect of his early humiliations, now sat across from him, radiating a calm that felt entirely alien to the jagged landscape of Elias’s current mood. Silas, with his impeccably tailored suit and eyes that seemed to absorb all available light, offered a small, almost imperceptible nod. "Welcome, Elias," Silas said, his voice a low, even current. Elias managed a curt nod in return, his jaw tight. He felt a strange, uncomfortable urge to pull his sleeves further over his hands, as if trying to shield himself from the scrutiny.

[story 2]

I kept my eyes down, focusing intently on the frayed edge of the magazine resting on my lap. The air in the office was too sterile, the muted tones amplifying the restless energy thrumming just beneath my skin. It had been years since I’d seen Marcus, the one who used to make my stomach clench with a mixture of fear and simmering resentment. Now, Marcus was Dr. Vance, and he was dissecting my anxieties with an unnerving precision. When he leaned forward, his expression softening into something that looked suspiciously like genuine empathy, I instinctively pulled my arms across my torso, a defensive gesture. "So," he began, his tone gentle but probing, "what is it that keeps you awake at night?" The question felt like an accusation. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my throat making the action difficult, and finally mumbled something vague, my gaze darting toward the window as if the view offered some form of escape.

[story 3]

The afternoon sun slanted through the blinds, striping the room in harsh, unforgiving lines. Liam sat rigidly on the edge of the visitor's chair, his knuckles white where he gripped the arms of the seat. He watched Dr. Vance as he spoke, absorbing every carefully chosen word. The air around Liam seemed charged, heavy with unspoken history. When Silas mentioned a childhood incident—one Liam had spent years trying to forget—a tremor ran through his hand, causing him to clench his fist so tightly his nails bit into his palm. He stared at the polished wood of the desk, his expression unreadable, a deep-seated tension radiating from him that mirrored the tightness coiled within his own chest. He didn't look up when Silas finished, just remained still, a statue carved from contained frustration.
